revision.text:
[[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Round Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq deprecated start]][[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[File:Hachimanyama kofun-Aichi02.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} in Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, is as large as a baseball field.
{{ill|National Land Aerial Photograph|ja|国土航空写真}}]] '''Enpun''' (円墳) refers to a type of tumulus with a circular plan, characteristic of [[kofun]]. == Overview == [[File:Gyoda Maruhakayama Tumulus In Spring from afar.jpg|thumb|230px|right|{{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}}, formerly regarded as the largest enpun]] Constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], enpun range from a few meters to over 100 meters in diameter, with most examples being medium- to small-sized. Due to its simple shape, changes in enpun morphology over time are not well-defined. Early and mid-period examples often feature a flat summit where a burial pit was excavated. Later examples from the terminal Kofun period tend to have dome-like shapes, narrower flat summits, and slightly higher mounds, with {{ill|yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|lt=corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Enpun with added projections are classified as {{ill|Hotategai-gata Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳|1=Scallop Kofun}}. While sometimes regarded as miniature {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, smaller examples are usually categorized as enpun.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 70.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 71. The largest enpun has long been considered the {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (diameter 105m) in the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. However, in 2017, the {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} in Nara City, previously measured at 86m in diameter, was found through surveys to be approximately 110m in diameter, making it the largest enpun.[http://www.city.nara.lg.jp/www/contents/1182152154399/index.html Kofun and Haniwa Kilns] - Nara City (accessed May 18, 2018, 5:50 JST). In comparison, the rear circle of the largest zenpō-kōen-fun, {{ill|Daisen Kofun|ja|大山古墳}}, measures 250m in diameter, and approximately 50 zenpō-kōen-fun have rear circles exceeding 100m.Refer to the "Enpun" entry by Taichirō Shiraishi in 'Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology,' edited by Takashi Tanaka and Makoto Sahara. == Regional Examples of Enpun == Below are representative enpun categorized by region, including names, readings, locations, periods, dimensions, and notable features: === Tōhoku and Hokkaidō === # {{ill|Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|ja|江釣子古墳群}} (Iwate Prefecture, Kitakami): Approximately 120 enpun, each about 10m in diameter, dating to the late Kofun period. Designated a National Historic Site.{{Cite book|author=Nobuo Takahashi|chapter=Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|title=Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 2: Early Times}} # {{ill|Takaseyama Kofun|ja|高瀬山古墳}} (Yamagata Prefecture, Sagae City): Diameter 34m, from the 7th century. A Yamagata Prefectural Historic Site. === Kantō === # {{ill|Kuruma-zuka Kofun (Mibu Town)|ja|車塚古墳 (壬生町)}} (Tochigi Prefecture, Mibu): Diameter 44.7m, National Historic Site. # {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (Saitama Prefecture, Gyōda): Diameter 105m, historically considered the largest enpun. Part of the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. === Chūbu and Tōkai === # {{ill|Kabutozuka Kofun (Iwata)|ja|兜塚古墳 (磐田市)}} (Shizuoka Prefecture, Iwata): Diameter 80m, largest in Shizuoka Prefecture. # {{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} (Aichi Prefecture, Nagoya): Diameter 74m, largest in the Tōkai region. Designated a National Historic Site in 1931. === Near Kyoto/Nara === # {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} (Nara City): Diameter approximately 110m, potentially the largest enpun in Japan. === Kyūshū === # {{ill|Gongen-zuka Kofun|ja|御塚・権現塚古墳}} (Fukuoka Prefecture, Kurume City): Diameter 50m, with a double moat and clay figurines. Designated a National Historic Site. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nagahara Keiji (ed.), Ishigami Eiichi (ed.). ''Iwanami Dictionary of Japanese History.'' Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 4-00-080093-0. * Takashi Tanaka, Makoto Sahara (eds.). ''Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology.'' Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 4-385-15835-5. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|List of Historic Sites in Japan|ja|日本の史跡一覧}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Round Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: categories]] [[Category:qq deprecated start]][[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[File:Hachimanyama kofun-Aichi02.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} in Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, is as large as a baseball field.
{{ill|National Land Aerial Photograph|ja|国土航空写真}}]] '''Enpun''' (円墳) refers to a type of tumulus with a circular plan, characteristic of [[kofun]]. == Overview == [[File:Gyoda Maruhakayama Tumulus In Spring from afar.jpg|thumb|230px|right|{{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}}, formerly regarded as the largest enpun]] Constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], enpun range from a few meters to over 100 meters in diameter, with most examples being medium- to small-sized. Due to its simple shape, changes in enpun morphology over time are not well-defined. Early and mid-period examples often feature a flat summit where a burial pit was excavated. Later examples from the terminal Kofun period tend to have dome-like shapes, narrower flat summits, and slightly higher mounds, with {{ill|yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|lt=corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Enpun with added projections are classified as {{ill|Hotategai-gata Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳|1=Scallop Kofun}}. While sometimes regarded as miniature {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, smaller examples are usually categorized as enpun.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 70.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 71. The largest enpun has long been considered the {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (diameter 105m) in the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. However, in 2017, the {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} in Nara City, previously measured at 86m in diameter, was found through surveys to be approximately 110m in diameter, making it the largest enpun.[http://www.city.nara.lg.jp/www/contents/1182152154399/index.html Kofun and Haniwa Kilns] - Nara City (accessed May 18, 2018, 5:50 JST). In comparison, the rear circle of the largest zenpō-kōen-fun, {{ill|Daisen Kofun|ja|大山古墳}}, measures 250m in diameter, and approximately 50 zenpō-kōen-fun have rear circles exceeding 100m.Refer to the "Enpun" entry by Taichirō Shiraishi in 'Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology,' edited by Takashi Tanaka and Makoto Sahara. == Regional Examples of Enpun == Below are representative enpun categorized by region, including names, readings, locations, periods, dimensions, and notable features: === Tōhoku and Hokkaidō === # {{ill|Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|ja|江釣子古墳群}} (Iwate Prefecture, Kitakami): Approximately 120 enpun, each about 10m in diameter, dating to the late Kofun period. Designated a National Historic Site.{{Cite book|author=Nobuo Takahashi|chapter=Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|title=Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 2: Early Times}} # {{ill|Takaseyama Kofun|ja|高瀬山古墳}} (Yamagata Prefecture, Sagae City): Diameter 34m, from the 7th century. A Yamagata Prefectural Historic Site. === Kantō === # {{ill|Kuruma-zuka Kofun (Mibu Town)|ja|車塚古墳 (壬生町)}} (Tochigi Prefecture, Mibu): Diameter 44.7m, National Historic Site. # {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (Saitama Prefecture, Gyōda): Diameter 105m, historically considered the largest enpun. Part of the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. === Chūbu and Tōkai === # {{ill|Kabutozuka Kofun (Iwata)|ja|兜塚古墳 (磐田市)}} (Shizuoka Prefecture, Iwata): Diameter 80m, largest in Shizuoka Prefecture. # {{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} (Aichi Prefecture, Nagoya): Diameter 74m, largest in the Tōkai region. Designated a National Historic Site in 1931. === Near Kyoto/Nara === # {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} (Nara City): Diameter approximately 110m, potentially the largest enpun in Japan. === Kyūshū === # {{ill|Gongen-zuka Kofun|ja|御塚・権現塚古墳}} (Fukuoka Prefecture, Kurume City): Diameter 50m, with a double moat and clay figurines. Designated a National Historic Site. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nagahara Keiji (ed.), Ishigami Eiichi (ed.). ''Iwanami Dictionary of Japanese History.'' Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 4-00-080093-0. * Takashi Tanaka, Makoto Sahara (eds.). ''Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology.'' Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 4-385-15835-5. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|List of Historic Sites in Japan|ja|日本の史跡一覧}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Kofun period]] While sometimes regarded as miniature {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, smaller examples are usually categorized as enpun.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 70.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 71. The largest enpun has long been considered the {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (diameter 105m) in the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. However, in 2017, the {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} in Nara City, previously measured at 86m in diameter, was found through surveys to be approximately 110m in diameter, making it the largest enpun.[http://www.city.nara.lg.jp/www/contents/1182152154399/index.html Kofun and Haniwa Kilns] - Nara City (accessed May 18, 2018, 5:50 JST). Designated a National Historic Site.{{Cite book|author=Nobuo Takahashi|chapter=Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|title=Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 2: Early Times}} # {{ill|Takaseyama Kofun|ja|高瀬山古墳}} (Yamagata Prefecture, Sagae City): Diameter 34m, from the 7th century. The largest enpun has long been considered the {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (diameter 105m) in the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. However, in 2017, the {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} in Nara City, previously measured at 86m in diameter, was found through surveys to be approximately 110m in diameter, making it the largest enpun.[http://www.city.nara.lg.jp/www/contents/1182152154399/index.html Kofun and Haniwa Kilns] - Nara City (accessed May 18, 2018, 5:50 JST). In comparison, the rear circle of the largest zenpō-kōen-fun, {{ill|Daisen Kofun|ja|大山古墳}}, measures 250m in diameter, and approximately 50 zenpō-kōen-fun have rear circles exceeding 100m.Refer to the "Enpun" entry by Taichirō Shiraishi in 'Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology,' edited by Takashi Tanaka and Makoto Sahara. == Regional Examples of Enpun == Below are representative enpun categorized by region, including names, readings, locations, periods, dimensions, and notable features: === Tōhoku and Hokkaidō === # {{ill|Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|ja|江釣子古墳群}} (Iwate Prefecture, Kitakami): Approximately 120 enpun, each about 10m in diameter, dating to the late Kofun period. Designated a National Historic Site.{{Cite book|author=Nobuo Takahashi|chapter=Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|title=Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 2: Early Times}} # {{ill|Takaseyama Kofun|ja|高瀬山古墳}} (Yamagata Prefecture, Sagae City): Diameter 34m, from the 7th century. A Yamagata Prefectural Historic Site. === Kantō === # {{ill|Kuruma-zuka Kofun (Mibu Town)|ja|車塚古墳 (壬生町)}} (Tochigi Prefecture, Mibu): Diameter 44.7m, National Historic Site. # {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (Saitama Prefecture, Gyōda): Diameter 105m, historically considered the largest enpun. Part of the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. === Chūbu and Tōkai === # {{ill|Kabutozuka Kofun (Iwata)|ja|兜塚古墳 (磐田市)}} (Shizuoka Prefecture, Iwata): Diameter 80m, largest in Shizuoka Prefecture. # {{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} (Aichi Prefecture, Nagoya): Diameter 74m, largest in the Tōkai region. Designated a National Historic Site in 1931. === Near Kyoto/Nara === # {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} (Nara City): Diameter approximately 110m, potentially the largest enpun in Japan. === Kyūshū === # {{ill|Gongen-zuka Kofun|ja|御塚・権現塚古墳}} (Fukuoka Prefecture, Kurume City): Diameter 50m, with a double moat and clay figurines. Designated a National Historic Site. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nagahara Keiji (ed.), Ishigami Eiichi (ed.). ''Iwanami Dictionary of Japanese History.'' Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 4-00-080093-0. * Takashi Tanaka, Makoto Sahara (eds.). ''Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology.'' Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 4-385-15835-5. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|List of Historic Sites in Japan|ja|日本の史跡一覧}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Round Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: categories]] [[File:Hachimanyama kofun-Aichi02.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} in Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, is as large as a baseball field.
{{ill|National Land Aerial Photograph|ja|国土航空写真}}]] '''Enpun''' (円墳) refers to a type of tumulus with a circular plan, characteristic of [[kofun]]. == Overview == [[File:Gyoda Maruhakayama Tumulus In Spring from afar.jpg|thumb|230px|right|{{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}}, formerly regarded as the largest enpun]] Constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], enpun range from a few meters to over 100 meters in diameter, with most examples being medium- to small-sized. Due to its simple shape, changes in enpun morphology over time are not well-defined. Early and mid-period examples often feature a flat summit where a burial pit was excavated. Later examples from the terminal Kofun period tend to have dome-like shapes, narrower flat summits, and slightly higher mounds, with {{ill|yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|lt=corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Enpun with added projections are classified as {{ill|Hotategai-gata Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳|1=Scallop Kofun}}. While sometimes regarded as miniature {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, smaller examples are usually categorized as enpun.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 70.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 71. The largest enpun has long been considered the {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (diameter 105m) in the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. However, in 2017, the {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} in Nara City, previously measured at 86m in diameter, was found through surveys to be approximately 110m in diameter, making it the largest enpun.[http://www.city.nara.lg.jp/www/contents/1182152154399/index.html Kofun and Haniwa Kilns] - Nara City (accessed May 18, 2018, 5:50 JST). In comparison, the rear circle of the largest zenpō-kōen-fun, {{ill|Daisen Kofun|ja|大山古墳}}, measures 250m in diameter, and approximately 50 zenpō-kōen-fun have rear circles exceeding 100m.Refer to the "Enpun" entry by Taichirō Shiraishi in 'Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology,' edited by Takashi Tanaka and Makoto Sahara. == Regional Examples of Enpun == Below are representative enpun categorized by region, including names, readings, locations, periods, dimensions, and notable features: === Tōhoku and Hokkaidō === # {{ill|Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|ja|江釣子古墳群}} (Iwate Prefecture, Kitakami): Approximately 120 enpun, each about 10m in diameter, dating to the late Kofun period. Designated a National Historic Site.{{Cite book|author=Nobuo Takahashi|chapter=Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|title=Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 2: Early Times}} # {{ill|Takaseyama Kofun|ja|高瀬山古墳}} (Yamagata Prefecture, Sagae City): Diameter 34m, from the 7th century. A Yamagata Prefectural Historic Site. === Kantō === # {{ill|Kuruma-zuka Kofun (Mibu Town)|ja|車塚古墳 (壬生町)}} (Tochigi Prefecture, Mibu): Diameter 44.7m, National Historic Site. # {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (Saitama Prefecture, Gyōda): Diameter 105m, historically considered the largest enpun. Part of the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. === Chūbu and Tōkai === # {{ill|Kabutozuka Kofun (Iwata)|ja|兜塚古墳 (磐田市)}} (Shizuoka Prefecture, Iwata): Diameter 80m, largest in Shizuoka Prefecture. # {{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} (Aichi Prefecture, Nagoya): Diameter 74m, largest in the Tōkai region. Designated a National Historic Site in 1931. === Near Kyoto/Nara === # {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} (Nara City): Diameter approximately 110m, potentially the largest enpun in Japan. === Kyūshū === # {{ill|Gongen-zuka Kofun|ja|御塚・権現塚古墳}} (Fukuoka Prefecture, Kurume City): Diameter 50m, with a double moat and clay figurines. Designated a National Historic Site. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nagahara Keiji (ed.), Ishigami Eiichi (ed.). ''Iwanami Dictionary of Japanese History.'' Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 4-00-080093-0. * Takashi Tanaka, Makoto Sahara (eds.). ''Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology.'' Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 4-385-15835-5. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|List of Historic Sites in Japan|ja|日本の史跡一覧}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Kofun period]] A Yamagata Prefectural Historic Site. === Kantō === # {{ill|Kuruma-zuka Kofun (Mibu Town)|ja|車塚古墳 (壬生町)}} (Tochigi Prefecture, Mibu): Diameter 44.7m, National Historic Site. # {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (Saitama Prefecture, Gyōda): Diameter 105m, historically considered the largest enpun. Part of the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. === Chūbu and Tōkai === # {{ill|Kabutozuka Kofun (Iwata)|ja|兜塚古墳 (磐田市)}} (Shizuoka Prefecture, Iwata): Diameter 80m, largest in Shizuoka Prefecture. # {{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} (Aichi Prefecture, Nagoya): Diameter 74m, largest in the Tōkai region. Designated a National Historic Site in 1931. === Near Kyoto/Nara === # {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} (Nara City): Diameter approximately 110m, potentially the largest enpun in Japan. === Kyūshū === # {{ill|Gongen-zuka Kofun|ja|御塚・権現塚古墳}} (Fukuoka Prefecture, Kurume City): Diameter 50m, with a double moat and clay figurines. Designated a National Historic Site. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nagahara Keiji (ed.), Ishigami Eiichi (ed.). ''Iwanami Dictionary of Japanese History.'' Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 4-00-080093-0. * Takashi Tanaka, Makoto Sahara (eds.). ''Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology.'' Sanseidō, 2003. [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Round Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: categories]] [[File:Hachimanyama kofun-Aichi02.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} in Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, is as large as a baseball field.
{{ill|National Land Aerial Photograph|ja|国土航空写真}}]] '''Enpun''' (円墳) refers to a type of tumulus with a circular plan, characteristic of [[kofun]]. == Overview == [[File:Gyoda Maruhakayama Tumulus In Spring from afar.jpg|thumb|230px|right|{{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}}, formerly regarded as the largest enpun]] Constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], enpun range from a few meters to over 100 meters in diameter, with most examples being medium- to small-sized. Due to its simple shape, changes in enpun morphology over time are not well-defined. Early and mid-period examples often feature a flat summit where a burial pit was excavated. Later examples from the terminal Kofun period tend to have dome-like shapes, narrower flat summits, and slightly higher mounds, with {{ill|yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|lt=corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Enpun with added projections are classified as {{ill|Hotategai-gata Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳|1=Scallop Kofun}}. While sometimes regarded as miniature {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, smaller examples are usually categorized as enpun.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 70.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 71. The largest enpun has long been considered the {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (diameter 105m) in the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. However, in 2017, the {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} in Nara City, previously measured at 86m in diameter, was found through surveys to be approximately 110m in diameter, making it the largest enpun.[http://www.city.nara.lg.jp/www/contents/1182152154399/index.html Kofun and Haniwa Kilns] - Nara City (accessed May 18, 2018, 5:50 JST). In comparison, the rear circle of the largest zenpō-kōen-fun, {{ill|Daisen Kofun|ja|大山古墳}}, measures 250m in diameter, and approximately 50 zenpō-kōen-fun have rear circles exceeding 100m.Refer to the "Enpun" entry by Taichirō Shiraishi in 'Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology,' edited by Takashi Tanaka and Makoto Sahara. == Regional Examples of Enpun == Below are representative enpun categorized by region, including names, readings, locations, periods, dimensions, and notable features: === Tōhoku and Hokkaidō === # {{ill|Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|ja|江釣子古墳群}} (Iwate Prefecture, Kitakami): Approximately 120 enpun, each about 10m in diameter, dating to the late Kofun period. Designated a National Historic Site.{{Cite book|author=Nobuo Takahashi|chapter=Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|title=Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 2: Early Times}} # {{ill|Takaseyama Kofun|ja|高瀬山古墳}} (Yamagata Prefecture, Sagae City): Diameter 34m, from the 7th century. A Yamagata Prefectural Historic Site. === Kantō === # {{ill|Kuruma-zuka Kofun (Mibu Town)|ja|車塚古墳 (壬生町)}} (Tochigi Prefecture, Mibu): Diameter 44.7m, National Historic Site. # {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (Saitama Prefecture, Gyōda): Diameter 105m, historically considered the largest enpun. Part of the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. === Chūbu and Tōkai === # {{ill|Kabutozuka Kofun (Iwata)|ja|兜塚古墳 (磐田市)}} (Shizuoka Prefecture, Iwata): Diameter 80m, largest in Shizuoka Prefecture. # {{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} (Aichi Prefecture, Nagoya): Diameter 74m, largest in the Tōkai region. Designated a National Historic Site in 1931. === Near Kyoto/Nara === # {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} (Nara City): Diameter approximately 110m, potentially the largest enpun in Japan. === Kyūshū === # {{ill|Gongen-zuka Kofun|ja|御塚・権現塚古墳}} (Fukuoka Prefecture, Kurume City): Diameter 50m, with a double moat and clay figurines. Designated a National Historic Site. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nagahara Keiji (ed.), Ishigami Eiichi (ed.). ''Iwanami Dictionary of Japanese History.'' Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 4-00-080093-0. * Takashi Tanaka, Makoto Sahara (eds.). ''Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology.'' Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 4-385-15835-5. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|List of Historic Sites in Japan|ja|日本の史跡一覧}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Round Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: categories]] [[File:Hachimanyama kofun-Aichi02.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} in Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, is as large as a baseball field.
{{ill|National Land Aerial Photograph|ja|国土航空写真}}]] '''Enpun''' (円墳) refers to a type of tumulus with a circular plan, characteristic of [[kofun]]. == Overview == [[File:Gyoda Maruhakayama Tumulus In Spring from afar.jpg|thumb|230px|right|{{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}}, formerly regarded as the largest enpun]] Constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], enpun range from a few meters to over 100 meters in diameter, with most examples being medium- to small-sized. Due to its simple shape, changes in enpun morphology over time are not well-defined. Early and mid-period examples often feature a flat summit where a burial pit was excavated. Later examples from the terminal Kofun period tend to have dome-like shapes, narrower flat summits, and slightly higher mounds, with {{ill|yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|lt=corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Enpun with added projections are classified as {{ill|Hotategai-gata Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳|1=Scallop Kofun}}. While sometimes regarded as miniature {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, smaller examples are usually categorized as enpun.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 70.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 71. The largest enpun has long been considered the {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (diameter 105m) in the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. However, in 2017, the {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} in Nara City, previously measured at 86m in diameter, was found through surveys to be approximately 110m in diameter, making it the largest enpun.[http://www.city.nara.lg.jp/www/contents/1182152154399/index.html Kofun and Haniwa Kilns] - Nara City (accessed May 18, 2018, 5:50 JST). In comparison, the rear circle of the largest zenpō-kōen-fun, {{ill|Daisen Kofun|ja|大山古墳}}, measures 250m in diameter, and approximately 50 zenpō-kōen-fun have rear circles exceeding 100m.Refer to the "Enpun" entry by Taichirō Shiraishi in 'Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology,' edited by Takashi Tanaka and Makoto Sahara. == Regional Examples of Enpun == Below are representative enpun categorized by region, including names, readings, locations, periods, dimensions, and notable features: === Tōhoku and Hokkaidō === # {{ill|Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|ja|江釣子古墳群}} (Iwate Prefecture, Kitakami): Approximately 120 enpun, each about 10m in diameter, dating to the late Kofun period. Designated a National Historic Site.{{Cite book|author=Nobuo Takahashi|chapter=Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|title=Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 2: Early Times}} # {{ill|Takaseyama Kofun|ja|高瀬山古墳}} (Yamagata Prefecture, Sagae City): Diameter 34m, from the 7th century. A Yamagata Prefectural Historic Site. === Kantō === # {{ill|Kuruma-zuka Kofun (Mibu Town)|ja|車塚古墳 (壬生町)}} (Tochigi Prefecture, Mibu): Diameter 44.7m, National Historic Site. # {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (Saitama Prefecture, Gyōda): Diameter 105m, historically considered the largest enpun. Part of the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. === Chūbu and Tōkai === # {{ill|Kabutozuka Kofun (Iwata)|ja|兜塚古墳 (磐田市)}} (Shizuoka Prefecture, Iwata): Diameter 80m, largest in Shizuoka Prefecture. # {{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} (Aichi Prefecture, Nagoya): Diameter 74m, largest in the Tōkai region. Designated a National Historic Site in 1931. === Near Kyoto/Nara === # {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} (Nara City): Diameter approximately 110m, potentially the largest enpun in Japan. === Kyūshū === # {{ill|Gongen-zuka Kofun|ja|御塚・権現塚古墳}} (Fukuoka Prefecture, Kurume City): Diameter 50m, with a double moat and clay figurines. Designated a National Historic Site. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nagahara Keiji (ed.), Ishigami Eiichi (ed.). ''Iwanami Dictionary of Japanese History.'' Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 4-00-080093-0. * Takashi Tanaka, Makoto Sahara (eds.). ''Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology.'' Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 4-385-15835-5. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|List of Historic Sites in Japan|ja|日本の史跡一覧}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Round Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: categories]] [[File:Hachimanyama kofun-Aichi02.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} in Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, is as large as a baseball field.
{{ill|National Land Aerial Photograph|ja|国土航空写真}}]] '''Enpun''' (円墳) refers to a type of tumulus with a circular plan, characteristic of [[kofun]]. == Overview == [[File:Gyoda Maruhakayama Tumulus In Spring from afar.jpg|thumb|230px|right|{{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}}, formerly regarded as the largest enpun]] Constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], enpun range from a few meters to over 100 meters in diameter, with most examples being medium- to small-sized. Due to its simple shape, changes in enpun morphology over time are not well-defined. Early and mid-period examples often feature a flat summit where a burial pit was excavated. Later examples from the terminal Kofun period tend to have dome-like shapes, narrower flat summits, and slightly higher mounds, with {{ill|yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|lt=corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Enpun with added projections are classified as {{ill|Hotategai-gata Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳|1=Scallop Kofun}}. While sometimes regarded as miniature {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, smaller examples are usually categorized as enpun.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 70.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 71. The largest enpun has long been considered the {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (diameter 105m) in the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. However, in 2017, the {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} in Nara City, previously measured at 86m in diameter, was found through surveys to be approximately 110m in diameter, making it the largest enpun.[http://www.city.nara.lg.jp/www/contents/1182152154399/index.html Kofun and Haniwa Kilns] - Nara City (accessed May 18, 2018, 5:50 JST). In comparison, the rear circle of the largest zenpō-kōen-fun, {{ill|Daisen Kofun|ja|大山古墳}}, measures 250m in diameter, and approximately 50 zenpō-kōen-fun have rear circles exceeding 100m.Refer to the "Enpun" entry by Taichirō Shiraishi in 'Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology,' edited by Takashi Tanaka and Makoto Sahara. == Regional Examples of Enpun == Below are representative enpun categorized by region, including names, readings, locations, periods, dimensions, and notable features: === Tōhoku and Hokkaidō === # {{ill|Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|ja|江釣子古墳群}} (Iwate Prefecture, Kitakami): Approximately 120 enpun, each about 10m in diameter, dating to the late Kofun period. Designated a National Historic Site.{{Cite book|author=Nobuo Takahashi|chapter=Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|title=Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 2: Early Times}} # {{ill|Takaseyama Kofun|ja|高瀬山古墳}} (Yamagata Prefecture, Sagae City): Diameter 34m, from the 7th century. A Yamagata Prefectural Historic Site. === Kantō === # {{ill|Kuruma-zuka Kofun (Mibu Town)|ja|車塚古墳 (壬生町)}} (Tochigi Prefecture, Mibu): Diameter 44.7m, National Historic Site. # {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (Saitama Prefecture, Gyōda): Diameter 105m, historically considered the largest enpun. Part of the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. === Chūbu and Tōkai === # {{ill|Kabutozuka Kofun (Iwata)|ja|兜塚古墳 (磐田市)}} (Shizuoka Prefecture, Iwata): Diameter 80m, largest in Shizuoka Prefecture. # {{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} (Aichi Prefecture, Nagoya): Diameter 74m, largest in the Tōkai region. Designated a National Historic Site in 1931. === Near Kyoto/Nara === # {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} (Nara City): Diameter approximately 110m, potentially the largest enpun in Japan. === Kyūshū === # {{ill|Gongen-zuka Kofun|ja|御塚・権現塚古墳}} (Fukuoka Prefecture, Kurume City): Diameter 50m, with a double moat and clay figurines. Designated a National Historic Site. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nagahara Keiji (ed.), Ishigami Eiichi (ed.). ''Iwanami Dictionary of Japanese History.'' Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 4-00-080093-0. * Takashi Tanaka, Makoto Sahara (eds.). ''Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology.'' Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 4-385-15835-5. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|List of Historic Sites in Japan|ja|日本の史跡一覧}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Round Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: categories]] [[File:Hachimanyama kofun-Aichi02.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} in Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, is as large as a baseball field.
{{ill|National Land Aerial Photograph|ja|国土航空写真}}]] '''Enpun''' (円墳) refers to a type of tumulus with a circular plan, characteristic of [[kofun]]. == Overview == [[File:Gyoda Maruhakayama Tumulus In Spring from afar.jpg|thumb|230px|right|{{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}}, formerly regarded as the largest enpun]] Constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], enpun range from a few meters to over 100 meters in diameter, with most examples being medium- to small-sized. Due to its simple shape, changes in enpun morphology over time are not well-defined. Early and mid-period examples often feature a flat summit where a burial pit was excavated. Later examples from the terminal Kofun period tend to have dome-like shapes, narrower flat summits, and slightly higher mounds, with {{ill|yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|lt=corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Enpun with added projections are classified as {{ill|Hotategai-gata Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳|1=Scallop Kofun}}. While sometimes regarded as miniature {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, smaller examples are usually categorized as enpun.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 70.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 71. The largest enpun has long been considered the {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (diameter 105m) in the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. However, in 2017, the {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} in Nara City, previously measured at 86m in diameter, was found through surveys to be approximately 110m in diameter, making it the largest enpun.[http://www.city.nara.lg.jp/www/contents/1182152154399/index.html Kofun and Haniwa Kilns] - Nara City (accessed May 18, 2018, 5:50 JST). In comparison, the rear circle of the largest zenpō-kōen-fun, {{ill|Daisen Kofun|ja|大山古墳}}, measures 250m in diameter, and approximately 50 zenpō-kōen-fun have rear circles exceeding 100m.Refer to the "Enpun" entry by Taichirō Shiraishi in 'Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology,' edited by Takashi Tanaka and Makoto Sahara. == Regional Examples of Enpun == Below are representative enpun categorized by region, including names, readings, locations, periods, dimensions, and notable features: === Tōhoku and Hokkaidō === # {{ill|Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|ja|江釣子古墳群}} (Iwate Prefecture, Kitakami): Approximately 120 enpun, each about 10m in diameter, dating to the late Kofun period. Designated a National Historic Site.{{Cite book|author=Nobuo Takahashi|chapter=Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|title=Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 2: Early Times}} # {{ill|Takaseyama Kofun|ja|高瀬山古墳}} (Yamagata Prefecture, Sagae City): Diameter 34m, from the 7th century. A Yamagata Prefectural Historic Site. === Kantō === # {{ill|Kuruma-zuka Kofun (Mibu Town)|ja|車塚古墳 (壬生町)}} (Tochigi Prefecture, Mibu): Diameter 44.7m, National Historic Site. # {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (Saitama Prefecture, Gyōda): Diameter 105m, historically considered the largest enpun. Part of the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. === Chūbu and Tōkai === # {{ill|Kabutozuka Kofun (Iwata)|ja|兜塚古墳 (磐田市)}} (Shizuoka Prefecture, Iwata): Diameter 80m, largest in Shizuoka Prefecture. # {{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} (Aichi Prefecture, Nagoya): Diameter 74m, largest in the Tōkai region. Designated a National Historic Site in 1931. === Near Kyoto/Nara === # {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} (Nara City): Diameter approximately 110m, potentially the largest enpun in Japan. === Kyūshū === # {{ill|Gongen-zuka Kofun|ja|御塚・権現塚古墳}} (Fukuoka Prefecture, Kurume City): Diameter 50m, with a double moat and clay figurines. Designated a National Historic Site. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nagahara Keiji (ed.), Ishigami Eiichi (ed.). ''Iwanami Dictionary of Japanese History.'' Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 4-00-080093-0. * Takashi Tanaka, Makoto Sahara (eds.). ''Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology.'' Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 4-385-15835-5. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|List of Historic Sites in Japan|ja|日本の史跡一覧}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Round Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: categories]] [[File:Hachimanyama kofun-Aichi02.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} in Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, is as large as a baseball field.
{{ill|National Land Aerial Photograph|ja|国土航空写真}}]] '''Enpun''' (円墳) refers to a type of tumulus with a circular plan, characteristic of [[kofun]]. == Overview == [[File:Gyoda Maruhakayama Kofun In Spring from afar.jpg|thumb|230px|right|{{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}}, formerly regarded as the largest enpun]] Constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], enpun range from a few meters to over 100 meters in diameter, with most examples being medium- to small-sized. Due to its simple shape, changes in enpun morphology over time are not well-defined. Early and mid-period examples often feature a flat summit where a burial pit was excavated. Later examples from the terminal Kofun period tend to have dome-like shapes, narrower flat summits, and slightly higher mounds, with {{ill|yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|lt=corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Enpun with added projections are classified as {{ill|Hotategai-gata Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳|1=Scallop Kofun}}. While sometimes regarded as miniature {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, smaller examples are usually categorized as enpun.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 70.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 71. The largest enpun has long been considered the {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (diameter 105m) in the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. However, in 2017, the {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} in Nara City, previously measured at 86m in diameter, was found through surveys to be approximately 110m in diameter, making it the largest enpun.[http://www.city.nara.lg.jp/www/contents/1182152154399/index.html Kofun and Haniwa Kilns] - Nara City (accessed May 18, 2018, 5:50 JST). In comparison, the rear circle of the largest zenpō-kōen-fun, {{ill|Daisen Kofun|ja|大山古墳}}, measures 250m in diameter, and approximately 50 zenpō-kōen-fun have rear circles exceeding 100m.Refer to the "Enpun" entry by Taichirō Shiraishi in 'Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology,' edited by Takashi Tanaka and Makoto Sahara. == Regional Examples of Enpun == Below are representative enpun categorized by region, including names, readings, locations, periods, dimensions, and notable features: === Tōhoku and Hokkaidō === # {{ill|Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|ja|江釣子古墳群}} (Iwate Prefecture, Kitakami): Approximately 120 enpun, each about 10m in diameter, dating to the late Kofun period. Designated a National Historic Site.{{Cite book|author=Nobuo Takahashi|chapter=Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|title=Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 2: Early Times}} # {{ill|Takaseyama Kofun|ja|高瀬山古墳}} (Yamagata Prefecture, Sagae City): Diameter 34m, from the 7th century. A Yamagata Prefectural Historic Site. === Kantō === # {{ill|Kuruma-zuka Kofun (Mibu Town)|ja|車塚古墳 (壬生町)}} (Tochigi Prefecture, Mibu): Diameter 44.7m, National Historic Site. # {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (Saitama Prefecture, Gyōda): Diameter 105m, historically considered the largest enpun. Part of the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. === Chūbu and Tōkai === # {{ill|Kabutozuka Kofun (Iwata)|ja|兜塚古墳 (磐田市)}} (Shizuoka Prefecture, Iwata): Diameter 80m, largest in Shizuoka Prefecture. # {{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} (Aichi Prefecture, Nagoya): Diameter 74m, largest in the Tōkai region. Designated a National Historic Site in 1931. === Near Kyoto/Nara === # {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} (Nara City): Diameter approximately 110m, potentially the largest enpun in Japan. === Kyūshū === # {{ill|Gongen-zuka Kofun|ja|御塚・権現塚古墳}} (Fukuoka Prefecture, Kurume City): Diameter 50m, with a double moat and clay figurines. Designated a National Historic Site. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nagahara Keiji (ed.), Ishigami Eiichi (ed.). ''Iwanami Dictionary of Japanese History.'' Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 4-00-080093-0. * Takashi Tanaka, Makoto Sahara (eds.). ''Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology.'' Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 4-385-15835-5. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|List of Historic Sites in Japan|ja|日本の史跡一覧}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Round Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category: categories]] [[File:Hachimanyama kofun-Aichi02.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} in Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, is as large as a baseball field.
{{ill|National Land Aerial Photograph|ja|国土航空写真}}]] '''Enpun''' (円墳) refers to a type of tumulus with a circular plan, characteristic of [[kofun]]. == Overview == [[File:Gyoda Maruhakayama Kofun In Spring from afar.jpg|thumb|230px|right|{{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}}, formerly regarded as the largest enpun]] Constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], enpun range from a few meters to over 100 meters in diameter, with most examples being medium- to small-sized. Due to its simple shape, changes in enpun morphology over time are not well-defined. Early and mid-period examples often feature a flat summit where a burial pit was excavated. Later examples from the terminal Kofun period tend to have dome-like shapes, narrower flat summits, and slightly higher mounds, with {{ill|yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|lt=corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Enpun with added projections are classified as {{ill|Hotategai-gata Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳|1=Scallop Kofun}}. While sometimes regarded as miniature {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, smaller examples are usually categorized as enpun.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 70.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 71. The largest enpun has long been considered the {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (diameter 105m) in the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. However, in 2017, the {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} in Nara City, previously measured at 86m in diameter, was found through surveys to be approximately 110m in diameter, making it the largest enpun.[http://www.city.nara.lg.jp/www/contents/1182152154399/index.html Kofun and Haniwa Kilns] - Nara City (accessed May 18, 2018, 5:50 JST). In comparison, the rear circle of the largest zenpō-kōen-fun, {{ill|Daisen Kofun|ja|大山古墳}}, measures 250m in diameter, and approximately 50 zenpō-kōen-fun have rear circles exceeding 100m.Refer to the "Enpun" entry by Taichirō Shiraishi in 'Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology,' edited by Takashi Tanaka and Makoto Sahara. == Regional Examples of Enpun == Below are representative enpun categorized by region, including names, readings, locations, periods, dimensions, and notable features: === Tōhoku and Hokkaidō === # {{ill|Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|ja|江釣子古墳群}} (Iwate Prefecture, Kitakami): Approximately 120 enpun, each about 10m in diameter, dating to the late Kofun period. Designated a National Historic Site.{{Cite book|author=Nobuo Takahashi|chapter=Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|title=Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 2: Early Times}} # {{ill|Takaseyama Kofun|ja|高瀬山古墳}} (Yamagata Prefecture, Sagae City): Diameter 34m, from the 7th century. A Yamagata Prefectural Historic Site. === Kantō === # {{ill|Kuruma-zuka Kofun (Mibu Town)|ja|車塚古墳 (壬生町)}} (Tochigi Prefecture, Mibu): Diameter 44.7m, National Historic Site. # {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (Saitama Prefecture, Gyōda): Diameter 105m, historically considered the largest enpun. Part of the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. === Chūbu and Tōkai === # {{ill|Kabutozuka Kofun (Iwata)|ja|兜塚古墳 (磐田市)}} (Shizuoka Prefecture, Iwata): Diameter 80m, largest in Shizuoka Prefecture. # {{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} (Aichi Prefecture, Nagoya): Diameter 74m, largest in the Tōkai region. Designated a National Historic Site in 1931. === Near Kyoto/Nara === # {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} (Nara City): Diameter approximately 110m, potentially the largest enpun in Japan. === Kyūshū === # {{ill|Gongen-zuka Kofun|ja|御塚・権現塚古墳}} (Fukuoka Prefecture, Kurume City): Diameter 50m, with a double moat and clay figurines. Designated a National Historic Site. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nagahara Keiji (ed.), Ishigami Eiichi (ed.). ''Iwanami Dictionary of Japanese History.'' Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 4-00-080093-0. * Takashi Tanaka, Makoto Sahara (eds.). ''Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology.'' Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 4-385-15835-5. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|List of Historic Sites in Japan|ja|日本の史跡一覧}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Kofun period]] Part of the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. === Chūbu and Tōkai === # {{ill|Kabutozuka Kofun (Iwata)|ja|兜塚古墳 (磐田市)}} (Shizuoka Prefecture, Iwata): Diameter 80m, largest in Shizuoka Prefecture. # {{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} (Aichi Prefecture, Nagoya): Diameter 74m, largest in the Tōkai region. Designated a National Historic Site in 1931. === Near Kyoto/Nara === # {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} (Nara City): Diameter approximately 110m, potentially the largest enpun in Japan. === Kyūshū === # {{ill|Gongen-zuka Kofun|ja|御塚・権現塚古墳}} (Fukuoka Prefecture, Kurume City): Diameter 50m, with a double moat and clay figurines. [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Round Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category:11]] [[File:Hachimanyama kofun-Aichi02.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} in Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, is as large as a baseball field.
{{ill|National Land Aerial Photograph|ja|国土航空写真}}]] '''Enpun''' (円墳) refers to a type of tumulus with a circular plan, characteristic of [[kofun]]. == Overview == [[File:Gyoda Maruhakayama Kofun In Spring from afar.jpg|thumb|230px|right|{{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}}, formerly regarded as the largest enpun]] Constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], enpun range from a few meters to over 100 meters in diameter, with most examples being medium- to small-sized. Due to its simple shape, changes in enpun morphology over time are not well-defined. Early and mid-period examples often feature a flat summit where a burial pit was excavated. Later examples from the terminal Kofun period tend to have dome-like shapes, narrower flat summits, and slightly higher mounds, with {{ill|yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|lt=corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Enpun with added projections are classified as {{ill|Hotategai-gata Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳|1=Scallop Kofun}}. While sometimes regarded as miniature {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, smaller examples are usually categorized as enpun.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 70.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 71. The largest enpun has long been considered the {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (diameter 105m) in the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. However, in 2017, the {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} in Nara City, previously measured at 86m in diameter, was found through surveys to be approximately 110m in diameter, making it the largest enpun.[http://www.city.nara.lg.jp/www/contents/1182152154399/index.html Kofun and Haniwa Kilns] - Nara City (accessed May 18, 2018, 5:50 JST). In comparison, the rear circle of the largest zenpō-kōen-fun, {{ill|Daisen Kofun|ja|大山古墳}}, measures 250m in diameter, and approximately 50 zenpō-kōen-fun have rear circles exceeding 100m.Refer to the "Enpun" entry by Taichirō Shiraishi in 'Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology,' edited by Takashi Tanaka and Makoto Sahara. == Regional Examples of Enpun == Below are representative enpun categorized by region, including names, readings, locations, periods, dimensions, and notable features: === Tōhoku and Hokkaidō === # {{ill|Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|ja|江釣子古墳群}} (Iwate Prefecture, Kitakami): Approximately 120 enpun, each about 10m in diameter, dating to the late Kofun period. Designated a National Historic Site.{{Cite book|author=Nobuo Takahashi|chapter=Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|title=Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 2: Early Times}} # {{ill|Takaseyama Kofun|ja|高瀬山古墳}} (Yamagata Prefecture, Sagae City): Diameter 34m, from the 7th century. A Yamagata Prefectural Historic Site. === Kantō === # {{ill|Kuruma-zuka Kofun (Mibu Town)|ja|車塚古墳 (壬生町)}} (Tochigi Prefecture, Mibu): Diameter 44.7m, National Historic Site. # {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (Saitama Prefecture, Gyōda): Diameter 105m, historically considered the largest enpun. Part of the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. === Chūbu and Tōkai === # {{ill|Kabutozuka Kofun (Iwata)|ja|兜塚古墳 (磐田市)}} (Shizuoka Prefecture, Iwata): Diameter 80m, largest in Shizuoka Prefecture. # {{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} (Aichi Prefecture, Nagoya): Diameter 74m, largest in the Tōkai region. Designated a National Historic Site in 1931. === Near Kyoto/Nara === # {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} (Nara City): Diameter approximately 110m, potentially the largest enpun in Japan. === Kyūshū === # {{ill|Gongen-zuka Kofun|ja|御塚・権現塚古墳}} (Fukuoka Prefecture, Kurume City): Diameter 50m, with a double moat and clay figurines. Designated a National Historic Site. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nagahara Keiji (ed.), Ishigami Eiichi (ed.). ''Iwanami Dictionary of Japanese History.'' Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 4-00-080093-0. * Takashi Tanaka, Makoto Sahara (eds.). ''Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology.'' Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 4-385-15835-5. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|List of Historic Sites in Japan|ja|日本の史跡一覧}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Kofun period]] The largest enpun has long been considered the {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (diameter 105m) in the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. However, in 2017, the {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} in Nara City, previously measured at 86m in diameter, was found through surveys to be approximately 110m in diameter, making it the largest enpun.[http://www.city.nara.lg.jp/www/contents/1182152154399/index.html Kofun and Haniwa Kilns] - Nara City (accessed May 18, 2018, 5:50 JST). In comparison, the rear circle of the largest zenpō-kōen-fun, {{ill|Daisen Kofun|ja|大山古墳}}, measures 250m in diameter, and approximately 50 zenpō-kōen-fun have rear circles exceeding 100m.Refer to the "Enpun" entry by Taichirō Shiraishi in 'Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology,' edited by Takashi Tanaka and Makoto Sahara. == Regional Examples of Enpun == Below are representative enpun categorized by region, including names, readings, locations, periods, dimensions, and notable features: === Tōhoku and Hokkaidō === # {{ill|Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|ja|江釣子古墳群}} (Iwate Prefecture, Kitakami): Approximately 120 enpun, each about 10m in diameter, dating to the late Kofun period. Designated a National Historic Site.{{Cite book|author=Nobuo Takahashi|chapter=Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|title=Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 2: Early Times}} # {{ill|Takaseyama Kofun|ja|高瀬山古墳}} (Yamagata Prefecture, Sagae City): Diameter 34m, from the 7th century. A Yamagata Prefectural Historic Site. === Kantō === # {{ill|Kuruma-zuka Kofun (Mibu Town)|ja|車塚古墳 (壬生町)}} (Tochigi Prefecture, Mibu): Diameter 44.7m, National Historic Site. # {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (Saitama Prefecture, Gyōda): Diameter 105m, historically considered the largest enpun. Part of the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. === Chūbu and Tōkai === # {{ill|Kabutozuka Kofun (Iwata)|ja|兜塚古墳 (磐田市)}} (Shizuoka Prefecture, Iwata): Diameter 80m, largest in Shizuoka Prefecture. # {{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} (Aichi Prefecture, Nagoya): Diameter 74m, largest in the Tōkai region. Designated a National Historic Site in 1931. === Near Kyoto/Nara === # {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} (Nara City): Diameter approximately 110m, potentially the largest enpun in Japan. === Kyūshū === # {{ill|Gongen-zuka Kofun|ja|御塚・権現塚古墳}} (Fukuoka Prefecture, Kurume City): Diameter 50m, with a double moat and clay figurines. Designated a National Historic Site. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nagahara Keiji (ed.), Ishigami Eiichi (ed.). ''Iwanami Dictionary of Japanese History.'' Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 4-00-080093-0. * Takashi Tanaka, Makoto Sahara (eds.). ''Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology.'' Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 4-385-15835-5. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|List of Historic Sites in Japan|ja|日本の史跡一覧}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Round Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category:Categories]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Round Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[File:Hachimanyama kofun-Aichi02.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} in Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, is as large as a baseball field.
{{ill|National Land Aerial Photograph|ja|国土航空写真}}]] '''Enpun''' (円墳) refers to a type of tumulus with a circular plan, characteristic of [[kofun]]. == Overview == [[File:Gyoda Maruhakayama Kofun In Spring from afar.jpg|thumb|230px|right|{{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}}, formerly regarded as the largest enpun]] Constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], enpun range from a few meters to over 100 meters in diameter, with most examples being medium- to small-sized. Due to its simple shape, changes in enpun morphology over time are not well-defined. Early and mid-period examples often feature a flat summit where a burial pit was excavated. Later examples from the terminal Kofun period tend to have dome-like shapes, narrower flat summits, and slightly higher mounds, with {{ill|yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|lt=corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Enpun with added projections are classified as {{ill|Hotategai-gata Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳|1=Scallop Kofun}}. While sometimes regarded as miniature {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, smaller examples are usually categorized as enpun.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 70.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 71. The largest enpun has long been considered the {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (diameter 105m) in the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. However, in 2017, the {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} in Nara City, previously measured at 86m in diameter, was found through surveys to be approximately 110m in diameter, making it the largest enpun.[http://www.city.nara.lg.jp/www/contents/1182152154399/index.html Kofun and Haniwa Kilns] - Nara City (accessed May 18, 2018, 5:50 JST). In comparison, the rear circle of the largest zenpō-kōen-fun, {{ill|Daisen Kofun|ja|大山古墳}}, measures 250m in diameter, and approximately 50 zenpō-kōen-fun have rear circles exceeding 100m.Refer to the "Enpun" entry by Taichirō Shiraishi in 'Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology,' edited by Takashi Tanaka and Makoto Sahara. == Regional Examples of Enpun == Below are representative enpun categorized by region, including names, readings, locations, periods, dimensions, and notable features: === Tōhoku and Hokkaidō === # {{ill|Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|ja|江釣子古墳群}} (Iwate Prefecture, Kitakami): Approximately 120 enpun, each about 10m in diameter, dating to the late Kofun period. Designated a National Historic Site.{{Cite book|author=Nobuo Takahashi|chapter=Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|title=Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 2: Early Times}} # {{ill|Takaseyama Kofun|ja|高瀬山古墳}} (Yamagata Prefecture, Sagae City): Diameter 34m, from the 7th century. A Yamagata Prefectural Historic Site. === Kantō === # {{ill|Kuruma-zuka Kofun (Mibu Town)|ja|車塚古墳 (壬生町)}} (Tochigi Prefecture, Mibu): Diameter 44.7m, National Historic Site. # {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (Saitama Prefecture, Gyōda): Diameter 105m, historically considered the largest enpun. Part of the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. === Chūbu and Tōkai === # {{ill|Kabutozuka Kofun (Iwata)|ja|兜塚古墳 (磐田市)}} (Shizuoka Prefecture, Iwata): Diameter 80m, largest in Shizuoka Prefecture. # {{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} (Aichi Prefecture, Nagoya): Diameter 74m, largest in the Tōkai region. Designated a National Historic Site in 1931. === Near Kyoto/Nara === # {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} (Nara City): Diameter approximately 110m, potentially the largest enpun in Japan. === Kyūshū === # {{ill|Gongen-zuka Kofun|ja|御塚・権現塚古墳}} (Fukuoka Prefecture, Kurume City): Diameter 50m, with a double moat and clay figurines. Designated a National Historic Site. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nagahara Keiji (ed.), Ishigami Eiichi (ed.). ''Iwanami Dictionary of Japanese History.'' Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 4-00-080093-0. * Takashi Tanaka, Makoto Sahara (eds.). ''Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology.'' Sanseidō, 2003. Later examples from the terminal Kofun period tend to have dome-like shapes, narrower flat summits, and slightly higher mounds, with {{ill|yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|lt=corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Enpun with added projections are classified as {{ill|Hotategai-gata Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳|1=Scallop Kofun}}. While sometimes regarded as miniature {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, smaller examples are usually categorized as enpun.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 70.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 71. The largest enpun has long been considered the {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (diameter 105m) in the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. However, in 2017, the {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} in Nara City, previously measured at 86m in diameter, was found through surveys to be approximately 110m in diameter, making it the largest enpun.[http://www.city.nara.lg.jp/www/contents/1182152154399/index.html Kofun and Haniwa Kilns] - Nara City (accessed May 18, 2018, 5:50 JST). In comparison, the rear circle of the largest zenpō-kōen-fun, {{ill|Daisen Kofun|ja|大山古墳}}, measures 250m in diameter, and approximately 50 zenpō-kōen-fun have rear circles exceeding 100m.Refer to the "Enpun" entry by Taichirō Shiraishi in 'Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology,' edited by Takashi Tanaka and Makoto Sahara. == Regional Examples of Enpun == Below are representative enpun categorized by region, including names, readings, locations, periods, dimensions, and notable features: === Tōhoku and Hokkaidō === # {{ill|Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|ja|江釣子古墳群}} (Iwate Prefecture, Kitakami): Approximately 120 enpun, each about 10m in diameter, dating to the late Kofun period. Designated a National Historic Site.{{Cite book|author=Nobuo Takahashi|chapter=Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|title=Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 2: Early Times}} # {{ill|Takaseyama Kofun|ja|高瀬山古墳}} (Yamagata Prefecture, Sagae City): Diameter 34m, from the 7th century. A Yamagata Prefectural Historic Site. === Kantō === # {{ill|Kuruma-zuka Kofun (Mibu Town)|ja|車塚古墳 (壬生町)}} (Tochigi Prefecture, Mibu): Diameter 44.7m, National Historic Site. # {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (Saitama Prefecture, Gyōda): Diameter 105m, historically considered the largest enpun. Part of the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. === Chūbu and Tōkai === # {{ill|Kabutozuka Kofun (Iwata)|ja|兜塚古墳 (磐田市)}} (Shizuoka Prefecture, Iwata): Diameter 80m, largest in Shizuoka Prefecture. # {{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} (Aichi Prefecture, Nagoya): Diameter 74m, largest in the Tōkai region. Designated a National Historic Site in 1931. === Near Kyoto/Nara === # {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} (Nara City): Diameter approximately 110m, potentially the largest enpun in Japan. === Kyūshū === # {{ill|Gongen-zuka Kofun|ja|御塚・権現塚古墳}} (Fukuoka Prefecture, Kurume City): Diameter 50m, with a double moat and clay figurines. Designated a National Historic Site. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nagahara Keiji (ed.), Ishigami Eiichi (ed.). ''Iwanami Dictionary of Japanese History.'' Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 4-00-080093-0. * Takashi Tanaka, Makoto Sahara (eds.). ''Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology.'' Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 4-385-15835-5. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|List of Historic Sites in Japan|ja|日本の史跡一覧}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:completion tier 4]][[sn:Round Kofun]][[Category:completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Round Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category:11]][[sn:Round Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[File:Hachimanyama kofun-Aichi02.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} in Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, is as large as a baseball field.
{{ill|National Land Aerial Photograph|ja|国土航空写真}}]] '''Enpun''' (円墳) refers to a type of tumulus with a circular plan, characteristic of [[kofun]]. == Overview == [[File:Gyoda Maruhakayama Kofun In Spring from afar.jpg|thumb|230px|right|{{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}}, formerly regarded as the largest enpun]] Constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], enpun range from a few meters to over 100 meters in diameter, with most examples being medium- to small-sized. Due to its simple shape, changes in enpun morphology over time are not well-defined. Early and mid-period examples often feature a flat summit where a burial pit was excavated. Later examples from the terminal Kofun period tend to have dome-like shapes, narrower flat summits, and slightly higher mounds, with {{ill|yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|lt=corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Enpun with added projections are classified as {{ill|Hotategai-gata Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳|1=Scallop Kofun}}. While sometimes regarded as miniature {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, smaller examples are usually categorized as enpun.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 70.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 71. The largest enpun has long been considered the {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (diameter 105m) in the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. However, in 2017, the {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} in Nara City, previously measured at 86m in diameter, was found through surveys to be approximately 110m in diameter, making it the largest enpun.[http://www.city.nara.lg.jp/www/contents/1182152154399/index.html Kofun and Haniwa Kilns] - Nara City (accessed May 18, 2018, 5:50 JST). In comparison, the rear circle of the largest zenpō-kōen-fun, {{ill|Daisen Kofun|ja|大山古墳}}, measures 250m in diameter, and approximately 50 zenpō-kōen-fun have rear circles exceeding 100m.Refer to the "Enpun" entry by Taichirō Shiraishi in 'Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology,' edited by Takashi Tanaka and Makoto Sahara. == Regional Examples of Enpun == Below are representative enpun categorized by region, including names, readings, locations, periods, dimensions, and notable features: === Tōhoku and Hokkaidō === # {{ill|Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|ja|江釣子古墳群}} (Iwate Prefecture, Kitakami): Approximately 120 enpun, each about 10m in diameter, dating to the late Kofun period. Designated a National Historic Site.{{Cite book|author=Nobuo Takahashi|chapter=Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|title=Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 2: Early Times}} # {{ill|Takaseyama Kofun|ja|高瀬山古墳}} (Yamagata Prefecture, Sagae City): Diameter 34m, from the 7th century. A Yamagata Prefectural Historic Site. === Kantō === # {{ill|Kuruma-zuka Kofun (Mibu Town)|ja|車塚古墳 (壬生町)}} (Tochigi Prefecture, Mibu): Diameter 44.7m, National Historic Site. # {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (Saitama Prefecture, Gyōda): Diameter 105m, historically considered the largest enpun. Part of the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. === Chūbu and Tōkai === # {{ill|Kabutozuka Kofun (Iwata)|ja|兜塚古墳 (磐田市)}} (Shizuoka Prefecture, Iwata): Diameter 80m, largest in Shizuoka Prefecture. # {{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} (Aichi Prefecture, Nagoya): Diameter 74m, largest in the Tōkai region. Designated a National Historic Site in 1931. === Near Kyoto/Nara === # {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} (Nara City): Diameter approximately 110m, potentially the largest enpun in Japan. === Kyūshū === # {{ill|Gongen-zuka Kofun|ja|御塚・権現塚古墳}} (Fukuoka Prefecture, Kurume City): Diameter 50m, with a double moat and clay figurines. Designated a National Historic Site. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nagahara Keiji (ed.), Ishigami Eiichi (ed.). ''Iwanami Dictionary of Japanese History.'' Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 4-00-080093-0. * Takashi Tanaka, Makoto Sahara (eds.). ''Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology.'' Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 4-385-15835-5. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|List of Historic Sites in Japan|ja|日本の史跡一覧}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category: tier 4]][[sn:Round Kofun]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category: tier 4]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Round Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category:11]][[sn:Round Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[File:Hachimanyama kofun-Aichi02.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} in Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, is as large as a baseball field.
{{ill|National Land Aerial Photograph|ja|国土航空写真}}]] '''Enpun''' (円墳) refers to a type of tumulus with a circular plan, characteristic of [[kofun]]. == Overview == [[File:Gyoda Maruhakayama Kofun In Spring from afar.jpg|thumb|230px|right|{{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}}, formerly regarded as the largest enpun]] Constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], enpun range from a few meters to over 100 meters in diameter, with most examples being medium- to small-sized. Due to its simple shape, changes in enpun morphology over time are not well-defined. Early and mid-period examples often feature a flat summit where a burial pit was excavated. Later examples from the terminal Kofun period tend to have dome-like shapes, narrower flat summits, and slightly higher mounds, with {{ill|yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|lt=corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Enpun with added projections are classified as {{ill|Hotategai-gata Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳|1=Scallop Kofun}}. While sometimes regarded as miniature {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, smaller examples are usually categorized as enpun.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 70.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 71. The largest enpun has long been considered the {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (diameter 105m) in the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. However, in 2017, the {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} in Nara City, previously measured at 86m in diameter, was found through surveys to be approximately 110m in diameter, making it the largest enpun.[http://www.city.nara.lg.jp/www/contents/1182152154399/index.html Kofun and Haniwa Kilns] - Nara City (accessed May 18, 2018, 5:50 JST). In comparison, the rear circle of the largest zenpō-kōen-fun, {{ill|Daisen Kofun|ja|大山古墳}}, measures 250m in diameter, and approximately 50 zenpō-kōen-fun have rear circles exceeding 100m.Refer to the "Enpun" entry by Taichirō Shiraishi in 'Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology,' edited by Takashi Tanaka and Makoto Sahara. == Regional Examples of Enpun == Below are representative enpun categorized by region, including names, readings, locations, periods, dimensions, and notable features: === Tōhoku and Hokkaidō === # {{ill|Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|ja|江釣子古墳群}} (Iwate Prefecture, Kitakami): Approximately 120 enpun, each about 10m in diameter, dating to the late Kofun period. Designated a National Historic Site.{{Cite book|author=Nobuo Takahashi|chapter=Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|title=Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 2: Early Times}} # {{ill|Takaseyama Kofun|ja|高瀬山古墳}} (Yamagata Prefecture, Sagae City): Diameter 34m, from the 7th century. A Yamagata Prefectural Historic Site. === Kantō === # {{ill|Kuruma-zuka Kofun (Mibu Town)|ja|車塚古墳 (壬生町)}} (Tochigi Prefecture, Mibu): Diameter 44.7m, National Historic Site. # {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (Saitama Prefecture, Gyōda): Diameter 105m, historically considered the largest enpun. Part of the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. === Chūbu and Tōkai === # {{ill|Kabutozuka Kofun (Iwata)|ja|兜塚古墳 (磐田市)}} (Shizuoka Prefecture, Iwata): Diameter 80m, largest in Shizuoka Prefecture. # {{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} (Aichi Prefecture, Nagoya): Diameter 74m, largest in the Tōkai region. Designated a National Historic Site in 1931. === Near Kyoto/Nara === # {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} (Nara City): Diameter approximately 110m, potentially the largest enpun in Japan. === Kyūshū === # {{ill|Gongen-zuka Kofun|ja|御塚・権現塚古墳}} (Fukuoka Prefecture, Kurume City): Diameter 50m, with a double moat and clay figurines. Designated a National Historic Site. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nagahara Keiji (ed.), Ishigami Eiichi (ed.). ''Iwanami Dictionary of Japanese History.'' Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 4-00-080093-0. * Takashi Tanaka, Makoto Sahara (eds.). ''Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology.'' Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 4-385-15835-5. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|List of Historic Sites in Japan|ja|日本の史跡一覧}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category: tier 4]][[sn:Round Kofun]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category: tier 4]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Round Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category:11]][[sn:Round Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[File:Hachimanyama kofun-Aichi02.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} in Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, is as large as a baseball field.
{{ill|National Land Aerial Photograph|ja|国土航空写真}}]] '''Enpun''' (円墳) refers to a type of tumulus with a circular plan, characteristic of [[kofun]]. == Overview == [[File:Gyoda Maruhakayama Kofun In Spring from afar.jpg|thumb|230px|right|{{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}}, formerly regarded as the largest enpun]] Constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], enpun range from a few meters to over 100 meters in diameter, with most examples being medium- to small-sized. Due to its simple shape, changes in enpun morphology over time are not well-defined. Early and mid-period examples often feature a flat summit where a burial pit was excavated. Later examples from the terminal Kofun period tend to have dome-like shapes, narrower flat summits, and slightly higher mounds, with {{ill|yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|lt=corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}. Enpun with added projections are classified as {{ill|Hotategai-gata Kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳|1=Scallop Kofun}}. While sometimes regarded as miniature {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, smaller examples are usually categorized as enpun.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 70.'Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), p. 71. The largest enpun has long been considered the {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (diameter 105m) in the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. However, in 2017, the {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} in Nara City, previously measured at 86m in diameter, was found through surveys to be approximately 110m in diameter, making it the largest enpun.[http://www.city.nara.lg.jp/www/contents/1182152154399/index.html Kofun and Haniwa Kilns] - Nara City (accessed May 18, 2018, 5:50 JST). In comparison, the rear circle of the largest zenpō-kōen-fun, {{ill|Daisen Kofun|ja|大山古墳}}, measures 250m in diameter, and approximately 50 zenpō-kōen-fun have rear circles exceeding 100m.Refer to the "Enpun" entry by Taichirō Shiraishi in 'Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology,' edited by Takashi Tanaka and Makoto Sahara. == Regional Examples of Enpun == Below are representative enpun categorized by region, including names, readings, locations, periods, dimensions, and notable features: === Tōhoku and Hokkaidō === # {{ill|Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|ja|江釣子古墳群}} (Iwate Prefecture, Kitakami): Approximately 120 enpun, each about 10m in diameter, dating to the late Kofun period. Designated a National Historic Site.{{Cite book|author=Nobuo Takahashi|chapter=Etsuriko Kofun Cluster|title=Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 2: Early Times}} # {{ill|Takaseyama Kofun|ja|高瀬山古墳}} (Yamagata Prefecture, Sagae City): Diameter 34m, from the 7th century. A Yamagata Prefectural Historic Site. === Kantō === # {{ill|Kuruma-zuka Kofun (Mibu Town)|ja|車塚古墳 (壬生町)}} (Tochigi Prefecture, Mibu): Diameter 44.7m, National Historic Site. # {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|ja|丸墓山古墳}} (Saitama Prefecture, Gyōda): Diameter 105m, historically considered the largest enpun. Part of the {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Cluster|ja|埼玉古墳群}}. === Chūbu and Tōkai === # {{ill|Kabutozuka Kofun (Iwata)|ja|兜塚古墳 (磐田市)}} (Shizuoka Prefecture, Iwata): Diameter 80m, largest in Shizuoka Prefecture. # {{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Nagoya)|ja|八幡山古墳 (名古屋市)}} (Aichi Prefecture, Nagoya): Diameter 74m, largest in the Tōkai region. Designated a National Historic Site in 1931. === Near Kyoto/Nara === # {{ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳}} (Nara City): Diameter approximately 110m, potentially the largest enpun in Japan. === Kyūshū === # {{ill|Gongen-zuka Kofun|ja|御塚・権現塚古墳}} (Fukuoka Prefecture, Kurume City): Diameter 50m, with a double moat and clay figurines. Designated a National Historic Site. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Nagahara Keiji (ed.), Ishigami Eiichi (ed.). ''Iwanami Dictionary of Japanese History.'' Iwanami Shoten, 1999. ISBN 4-00-080093-0. * Takashi Tanaka, Makoto Sahara (eds.). ''Dictionary of Japanese Archaeology.'' Sanseidō, 2003. ISBN 4-385-15835-5. == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|List of Historic Sites in Japan|ja|日本の史跡一覧}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Kofun period]]
